content.body:
Nucleus: protons (Z) + neutrons (N)
Mass number A = Z + N
Nuclide notation: ᴬ_Z X

Isotopes: same Z, different N

Decay equations (conserve A and Z):
Alpha: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ⁻⁴_{Z-2} Y + ⁴_₂He
Beta-minus: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_{Z+1} Y + ⁰_{-1}e + antineutrino
Beta-plus: ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_{Z-1} Y + ⁰_{+1}e + neutrino
Gamma: no change in A or Z, just energy release

content.title: Decay Examples
content.body:
²³⁸_₉₂U → ²³⁴_₉₀Th + ⁴_₂He (alpha decay)
¹⁴_₆C → ¹⁴_₇N + ⁰_{-1}e (beta-minus: carbon-14 dating)
⁶⁰_₂₇Co → ⁶⁰_₂₈Ni + ⁰_{-1}e + γ (beta + gamma)

Penetrating power:
Alpha (⁴He): stopped by paper/few cm air
Beta (electron): stopped by few mm aluminium
Gamma (γ-ray): reduced by thick lead/concrete

Binding energy:
E = mc²  (Einstein mass-energy)
Mass defect Δm: mass of nucleus < sum of constituent masses
Binding energy = Δm × c²
Higher binding energy per nucleon = more stable nucleus
Fe-56: most stable nucleus

questions.question: Mass number A is the total number of
questions.explanation: A = Z (protons) + N (neutrons) = total nucleons

questions.question: Isotopes have the same
options: number of electrons in outer shell
questions.explanation: Isotopes: same element (same Z) but different N → different A

questions.question: Alpha decay of ²³⁸_₉₂U produces
questions.explanation: Alpha particle is ⁴_₂He: A decreases by 4, Z decreases by 2. 238→234, 92→90 = Th

questions.question: Beta-minus decay converts a neutron to
questions.explanation: β⁻ decay: n → p + e⁻ + antineutrino. Z increases by 1

questions.question: Most penetrating radiation is
questions.explanation: Gamma: electromagnetic radiation, no charge. Stopped only by thick lead or concrete

questions.question: Alpha particles are stopped by
questions.explanation: Alpha: heavy, doubly charged. Stopped by paper or a few centimetres of air

questions.question: E=mc² means
options: speed of light squared is constant
questions.explanation: Einstein's mass-energy equivalence: a mass Δm converts to enormous energy E=Δmc²

questions.question: Why is Fe-56 the most stable nucleus?
questions.explanation: Fe-56 sits at peak of binding energy per nucleon curve — maximum stability

questions.question: Gamma decay results in
options: no change in A or Z (just energy release)
questions.answer: no change in A or Z (just energy release)
questions.explanation: Gamma: nucleus releases excess energy as photon. Proton and neutron numbers unchanged

questions.question: Carbon-14 (¹⁴_₆C) undergoes beta-minus decay. Product is
questions.explanation: Beta-minus: Z increases by 1 (6→7=Nitrogen), A unchanged (14). Product: ¹⁴_₇N

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Decay law:
N = N₀ e^(-λt)
A = A₀ e^(-λt)  (activity = λN)
Half-life T½ = ln2/λ = 0.693/λ

N₀=initial nuclei, N=nuclei at time t
λ=decay constant (s⁻¹)
Activity A = dN/dt = λN  (in Becquerel, Bq)
1 Bq = 1 decay per second
1 Ci (Curie) = 3.7×10¹⁰ Bq

content.title: Half-life Problems
content.body:
¹⁴C: T½=5730 years, λ=0.693/5730=1.21×10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹
Fraction remaining after 11460 years (2 half-lives):
N/N₀ = (½)² = 0.25 → 25% remains

I-131: T½=8 days. Initial activity 10,000 Bq.
After 24 days (3 half-lives): A=10000×(½)³=1250 Bq

content.title: Carbon Dating
content.body:
Living organisms maintain constant ¹⁴C ratio
At death, ¹⁴C decays with T½=5730 years
Measure ¹⁴C ratio → calculate age
Useful for ~50,000 year range
Older: use ⁴⁰K/⁴⁰Ar (T½=1.26 Gyr) or U-Pb

questions.explanation: T½=ln2/λ=0.693/λ where λ is the decay constant

N=N₀e^(-λt). After one half-life, N/N₀=
questions.explanation: By definition of half-life: half the nuclei remain after one T½

questions.explanation: Activity A=λN (decay constant × number of undecayed nuclei)

questions.explanation: 1 Bq = 1 nuclear disintegration per second

questions.answer: ¹⁴C activity too low to measure accurately
questions.explanation: After ~8-9 half-lives, remaining ¹⁴C < 0.2% — below detection limits of most instruments

Radioactive decay is random and spontaneous, meaning
options: we can predict exactly when each nucleus decays
we can only predict statistically, not individually
questions.answer: we can only predict statistically, not individually
questions.explanation: Individual decay: unpredictable. Large numbers: statistically predictable half-life. Temperature/pressure don't affect it

questions.question: Which isotope is best for dating rocks billions of years old?
questions.explanation: Match half-life to age range: ²³⁸U half-life similar to rock ages (billions of years) → good precision

content.body:
Nuclear fission:
Heavy nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei + neutrons + energy
²³⁵U + n → ⁹²Kr + ¹⁴¹Ba + 3n + 200MeV
Chain reaction: neutrons from fission cause more fissions
Critical mass: minimum mass for self-sustaining chain reaction

Nuclear fusion:
Light nuclei combine to form heavier nucleus + energy
²₁H + ³₁H → ⁴₂He + n + 17.6 MeV
Requires extreme temperature (>10⁷K) to overcome Coulomb barrier

content.title: Nuclear Power
content.body:
Controlled fission in reactor:
Fuel: enriched ²³⁵U (3-5%)
Moderator (graphite/water): slows neutrons to thermal energies
Control rods (boron): absorb neutrons, control reaction rate
Coolant: transfers heat to steam turbine
Shielding: concrete blocks radiation
1kg ²³⁵U releases ~80 million times energy of 1kg coal

content.title: Fusion Power
content.body:
Sun: H→He fusion, 4.6×10⁹ years
Attempts on Earth: tokamak (magnetic confinement), laser fusion
JET, ITER projects
Advantages: more energy than fission, fuel (deuterium from sea) abundant, less radioactive waste
Challenge: sustaining plasma at 10⁸K

questions.question: Nuclear fission releases energy because
options: product nuclei have lower total mass (mass defect→E=mc²)
questions.answer: product nuclei have lower total mass (mass defect→E=mc²)
questions.explanation: Mass defect: products slightly lighter than reactants → mass converted to energy via E=mc²

questions.question: Chain reaction occurs when fission neutrons cause
questions.explanation: Each fission releases ~3 neutrons which can each cause new fissions → exponential chain reaction

questions.question: Control rods in nuclear reactor
options: absorb neutrons to control reaction rate
questions.answer: absorb neutrons to control reaction rate
questions.explanation: Control rods (boron/cadmium) absorb neutrons — inserting further slows/stops reaction

questions.question: Moderator in nuclear reactor slows neutrons to
options: thermal (slow) speeds so they are better absorbed by ²³⁵U
questions.answer: thermal (slow) speeds so they are better absorbed by ²³⁵U
questions.explanation: Fast neutrons less likely to cause fission. Moderator (water/graphite) thermalises neutrons → more efficient fission

questions.question: Nuclear fusion requires extremely high temperature to
questions.explanation: Nuclei both positively charged → repel. Need enormous KE (10⁸K) to get close enough for strong nuclear force

questions.question: Fusion produces more energy per kg of fuel than fission because
options: fusion releases more energy per nucleon at the binding energy curve peak region
questions.answer: fusion releases more energy per nucleon at the binding energy curve peak region
questions.explanation: Light nuclei (H→He) have steep increase in binding energy/nucleon → large energy release

questions.question: Main advantage of fusion over fission
questions.explanation: Fusion products (helium) not radioactive; no long-lived nuclear waste. Fuel is deuterium from sea

questions.question: Critical mass in fission is
options: minimum mass for self-sustaining chain reaction
questions.answer: minimum mass for self-sustaining chain reaction
questions.explanation: Below critical mass: too many neutrons escape without causing fission. At/above: chain reaction self-sustains

questions.question: Enriched uranium contains higher proportion of
questions.explanation: Natural uranium is 99.3% ²³⁸U, 0.7% ²³⁵U. Enrichment increases ²³⁵U (fissile) fraction to 3-90%

questions.question: ²₁H + ³₁H → ⁴₂He + ?
questions.explanation: Deuterium-tritium fusion: ²₁H+³₁H → ⁴₂He + ¹₀n (neutron) + 17.6 MeV. Conserve A(2+3=4+1) and Z(1+1=2+0)✓

content.body:
Radiation units:
Activity: Becquerel (Bq) = decays/second
Absorbed dose: Gray (Gy) = J/kg
Effective dose: Sievert (Sv) = Gy × radiation weighting factor
α has weighting 20, β and γ have weighting 1

Background radiation sources:
~50% radon gas (geology)
~15% medical (X-rays)
~12% cosmic rays
~12% food/drink
~11% building materials
UK average: ~2.7 mSv/year

content.title: Medical Applications
content.body:
Diagnostic:
X-rays: bone imaging (dense tissue absorbs more)
CT scan: many X-ray angles
PET scan: ¹⁸F-FDG positron emitter → gamma detected

Therapy:
Radiotherapy: γ-rays kill cancer cells
¹³¹I for thyroid cancer (selectively absorbed)
Brachytherapy: radioactive seeds implanted

content.title: Industrial Applications
content.body:
Thickness gauging: β or γ passes through material, detector measures transmission — more passes = thinner material
Smoke detectors: ²⁴¹Am α emitter ionises air in detector
Sterilisation: γ rays kill bacteria in medical equipment/food
Tracer techniques: radioactive isotope follows flow (pipelines, groundwater)

questions.question: Unit of absorbed radiation dose is
questions.explanation: Absorbed dose in Gray: energy deposited per kg of tissue

questions.question: Sievert accounts for
options: absorbed dose × radiation weighting factor (biological damage)
questions.answer: absorbed dose × radiation weighting factor (biological damage)
questions.explanation: Effective dose (Sv) = Gy × radiation weighting factor. α causes 20× more biological damage than γ per Gy

questions.question: Alpha radiation weighting factor is 20, meaning
options: alpha causes 20× more biological damage per J/kg than gamma
questions.answer: alpha causes 20× more biological damage per J/kg than gamma
questions.explanation: Alpha particles are highly ionising — cause much more cellular damage than beta or gamma for same absorbed energy

questions.question: Largest source of background radiation in many countries is
questions.explanation: Radon-222 (from uranium decay in granite rocks) accounts for ~50% of average background dose

questions.question: ¹³¹I (iodine-131) is used for thyroid cancer because
options: thyroid selectively absorbs iodine → targeted irradiation
questions.answer: thyroid selectively absorbs iodine → targeted irradiation
questions.explanation: Thyroid naturally concentrates iodine. ¹³¹I delivers targeted radiation (β and γ) directly to thyroid tissue

questions.question: Smoke detectors use americium-241 because
options: it emits alpha particles that ionise air between detector plates
questions.answer: it emits alpha particles that ionise air between detector plates
questions.explanation: ²⁴¹Am α emitter: ionises air creating small current. Smoke disrupts ionisation → alarm triggers

questions.question: ALARA radiation safety principle means
questions.explanation: ALARA: keep radiation dose As Low As Reasonably Achievable through time, distance, shielding

questions.question: PET scan uses positron emitters. Positrons annihilate with electrons producing
questions.explanation: Positron+electron → 2γ photons (511 keV each, opposite directions) detected to locate source in body

questions.question: Gamma irradiation of food (sterilisation) makes food
questions.explanation: Gamma irradiation kills bacteria/insects without making food radioactive — used for spices, some produce

questions.question: Time, distance and shielding reduce radiation dose because
options: time: less exposure; distance: inverse square law I∝1/r²; shielding: absorbs radiation
questions.answer: time: less exposure; distance: inverse square law I∝1/r²; shielding: absorbs radiation
questions.explanation: Three principles of radiation protection: minimise time exposed, maximise distance, use appropriate shielding

content.body:
Radioactive decay is random and spontaneous.

Half-life (t½): time for half the nuclei to decay

N = N₀ × (½)^(t/t½)
A = A₀ × (½)^(t/t½)  (activity)

Decay constant λ: N = N₀e^(-λt)
λ = ln2 / t½ = 0.693 / t½
Activity A = λN

Units: Becquerel (Bq) = 1 decay/second

content.title: Half-Life Calculations
content.body:
Sample: N₀=1000, t½=3 years
After 3y: N=500
After 6y: N=250
After 9y: N=125
After 12y: N=62.5

Formula: N=1000×(½)^(12/3)=1000×(½)^4=1000/16=62.5

content.title: Carbon-14 Dating
content.body:
¹⁴C t½=5730 years. Cosmic rays continually make ¹⁴C in atmosphere.
Living organisms maintain constant ¹⁴C ratio.
After death, ¹⁴C decays. Measure remaining ratio → calculate age.

Bone has 25% original ¹⁴C:
(½)^(t/5730)=0.25=(½)^2 → t=2×5730=11,460 years

questions.question: Half-life is defined as
options: time for ALL nuclei to decay
time for HALF the nuclei to decay
time for activity to reach zero
questions.answer: time for HALF the nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: t½: the time during which the number of radioactive nuclei (and activity) halves

questions.question: N₀=800, t½=2 hours. After 6 hours, N=
Decay is described as random because
options: we cannot predict WHICH nucleus will decay next
questions.answer: we cannot predict WHICH nucleus will decay next
questions.explanation: Radioactive decay is a quantum process — individually unpredictable, statistically predictable in bulk

questions.question: Activity A = λN. If λ=0.1 s⁻¹ and N=5000, A=
questions.explanation: A=λN=0.1×5000=500 decays per second = 500 Bq

questions.question: t½=10 days. Decay constant λ=
After 4 half-lives, fraction remaining=
Carbon-14 dating works because
options: living organisms maintain constant ¹⁴C/¹²C ratio which decays after death
questions.answer: living organisms maintain constant ¹⁴C/¹²C ratio which decays after death
questions.explanation: While alive: ¹⁴C replenished from atmosphere. After death: ¹⁴C decays, ratio decreases predictably

questions.question: Technetium-99m (t½=6 hours) used in medical scans. After 24 hours, fraction remaining=
Background radiation comes from
options: cosmic rays, rocks (radon), food, medical sources
questions.answer: cosmic rays, rocks (radon), food, medical sources
questions.explanation: Background radiation: ~50% from radon gas, ~10% cosmic, ~12% medical, ~13% food, ~15% buildings/soil

questions.question: N=N₀e^(-λt). Why does activity never reach exactly zero?
options: Exponential decay approaches zero asymptotically but never reaches it
questions.answer: Exponential decay approaches zero asymptotically but never reaches it
questions.explanation: Exponential function: e^(-λt)→0 as t→∞ but never equals zero mathematically

content.body:
Nuclear Fission:
Heavy nucleus splits into smaller nuclei + neutrons + energy
²³⁵U + n → ⁹²Kr + ¹⁴¹Ba + 3n + 200 MeV
Chain reaction: neutrons cause further fissions
Critical mass: minimum mass for self-sustaining chain reaction

Nuclear Fusion:
Light nuclei combine → heavier nucleus + energy
²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV
Requires extreme T (~10⁸ K) and pressure
Powers the sun and stars

content.title: Nuclear Power Station
content.body:
Fuel: enriched uranium (3% ²³⁵U)
Moderation: slow neutrons (graphite/water)
Control rods: absorb neutrons (boron/cadmium) — control reaction rate
Coolant: removes heat (water/CO₂/liquid metal)
Heat exchanger → steam → turbine → generator
Containment: thick concrete shields radiation

content.title: Fusion vs Fission
content.body:
Fission advantages: controllable, lots of energy per kg
Fission disadvantages: radioactive waste (thousands of years), meltdown risk

Fusion advantages: more energy per reaction, fuel abundant (hydrogen from sea), little radioactive waste
Fusion disadvantages: requires 100 million°C — no material can contain it directly (magnetic confinement: tokamak)

questions.question: Nuclear fission involves
options: splitting heavy nucleus into smaller fragments + energy
questions.answer: splitting heavy nucleus into smaller fragments + energy
questions.explanation: Fission: heavy nucleus (U-235, Pu-239) splits when struck by neutron, releasing 2-3 neutrons and ~200 MeV

questions.question: In a chain reaction, neutrons from one fission cause
questions.explanation: Each fission releases ~3 neutrons; each can trigger another fission → exponential chain reaction

questions.question: Control rods in a nuclear reactor
options: absorb neutrons to control reaction rate
questions.answer: absorb neutrons to control reaction rate
questions.explanation: Control rods (boron/cadmium) absorb neutrons. Inserting deeper → fewer neutrons available → reaction slows

questions.question: Moderator in a nuclear reactor slows neutrons because
options: fast neutrons cause too much fission
slow (thermal) neutrons are more likely to cause fission in U-235
questions.answer: slow (thermal) neutrons are more likely to cause fission in U-235
questions.explanation: U-235 fission cross-section is much higher for thermal (slow) neutrons — moderation improves efficiency

questions.question: Nuclear fusion powers the sun. The reaction fuses
questions.explanation: Sun: p-p chain fusing hydrogen → helium. Fusion reactors use ²H+³H→⁴He+n+17.6 MeV

questions.question: Main challenge for controlled fusion reactors on Earth
options: plasma must reach ~100 million°C — containing it is extremely difficult
questions.answer: plasma must reach ~100 million°C — containing it is extremely difficult
questions.explanation: Fusion requires T>10⁸K. No material withstands this — magnetic confinement (tokamak) or inertial confinement used

questions.question: Radioactive waste from fission requires
options: safe storage for thousands of years
questions.answer: safe storage for thousands of years
questions.explanation: Long-lived isotopes (Cs-137 t½=30y, Pu-239 t½=24,000y) require deep geological storage for millennia

questions.question: Fusion is considered cleaner than fission because
options: fuel is unlimited hydrogen from seawater and products are mainly helium (not long-lived waste)
questions.answer: fuel is unlimited hydrogen from seawater and products are mainly helium (not long-lived waste)
questions.explanation: Fusion: abundant fuel (H from seawater), products mostly ⁴He (stable) — far less problematic waste than fission

Critical mass of fissile material is the minimum mass needed for
questions.explanation: Below critical mass: too many neutrons escape without causing fission. At/above critical: chain reaction sustained

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Ionising radiation can damage living tissue. Understanding risks and protective measures is essential. Medical applications exploit controlled radiation for diagnosis and treatment.

Radiation dose:
Absorbed dose: D = E/m (Gray, Gy = J/kg)
Equivalent dose: H = D × Q (Sievert, Sv)
Q = quality factor (α=20, β=1, γ=1)
Effective dose accounts for tissue sensitivity

Protection: Time, Distance, Shielding
Inverse square law: I ∝ 1/r²

content.title: Medical Uses
content.body:
Diagnosis:
• X-rays: dense tissue (bones) absorb more → shadow image
• CT scan: multiple X-ray angles → 3D image
• PET scan: positron-emitting isotope → annihilation γ-rays
• Gamma camera: Tc-99m (t½=6h) γ-emission tracked

Therapy:
• Radiotherapy: γ-rays (Co-60) targeted at tumour
• Brachytherapy: radioactive seeds implanted
• Iodine-131: treats thyroid cancer (absorbed by thyroid)

content.title: Radiation Protection
content.body:
ALARA principle: As Low As Reasonably Achievable
Time: minimize exposure time
Distance: double distance → ¼ intensity (inverse square)
Shielding: α=paper, β=aluminium, γ=thick lead/concrete

Radon gas: naturally occurring, can accumulate indoors
Most significant natural radiation source in UK homes

questions.question: Unit of absorbed radiation dose is
questions.explanation: Gray (Gy): absorbed dose = energy deposited per unit mass (J/kg). Sievert accounts for biological effect

questions.question: Alpha particles are most ionising inside body because
options: they have large mass and charge, depositing all energy over short distance
questions.answer: they have large mass and charge, depositing all energy over short distance
questions.explanation: Alpha: mass=4, charge=2 → intense ionisation in very short path (~40μm in tissue) — most damaging internally

questions.question: Inverse square law: double the distance from source, intensity becomes
questions.explanation: I∝1/r². Double r → I×(1/4). Essential for radiation safety

questions.question: Technetium-99m (t½=6h) is ideal for medical scans because
options: it emits only gamma (for detection), short half-life (reduces patient dose), and concentrates in specific tissues
questions.answer: it emits only gamma (for detection), short half-life (reduces patient dose), and concentrates in specific tissues
questions.explanation: Ideal medical isotope: gamma emitter (detectable outside body), short t½, chemically attaches to target tissue

questions.question: Radiotherapy treats cancer by
options: targeted ionising radiation damaging tumour DNA
questions.answer: targeted ionising radiation damaging tumour DNA
questions.explanation: Radiation breaks DNA strands in rapidly dividing cancer cells. Multiple beams cross at tumour to minimise healthy tissue dose

questions.question: ALARA principle in radiation protection means
options: keep dose as low as reasonably achievable
questions.answer: keep dose as low as reasonably achievable
questions.explanation: ALARA: minimize exposure while achieving required purpose — time, distance, shielding

questions.question: Main advantage of PET scan over X-ray
options: gives 3D metabolic activity images (functional, not just structural)
questions.answer: gives 3D metabolic activity images (functional, not just structural)
questions.explanation: PET: positron-emitting tracer shows metabolic activity (e.g. glucose uptake) — can detect cancer before structural changes

questions.question: Radon gas is dangerous because
options: it is an alpha emitter that can be inhaled, irradiating lung tissue
questions.answer: it is an alpha emitter that can be inhaled, irradiating lung tissue
questions.explanation: Radon-222 (from uranium decay in rocks) enters buildings. When inhaled, alpha decay directly irradiates bronchial tissue

questions.question: Worker receives 2 Gy of alpha radiation (Q=20). Equivalent dose =
questions.explanation: H=D×Q=2×20=40 Sv (very dangerous — lethal dose ~6Sv whole body)

questions.question: Background radiation sources in Uganda/Africa include
questions.explanation: Natural background: cosmic rays, terrestrial (U-238, Th-232 in rocks), radon, food (K-40, C-14). Plus medical exposures

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.
